questions.explanation: Virchow's triad: (1) Endothelial injury (most important — atherosclerosis, trauma, inflammation), (2) Stasis or turbulence of blood flow (immobilization, atrial fibrillation, aneurysm), (3) Hypercoagulability (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, antiphospholipid syndrome, malignancy, oral contraceptives). DVT → pulmonary embolism (most common cause of PE). Reference: Robbins — Thrombosis.

questions.questionText: The most common source of pulmonary thromboembolism is:
questions.optionB: Deep veins of the lower extremities (DVT — iliofemoral veins)
questions.explanation: Pulmonary embolism: >95% arise from DVT of deep veins of lower extremities (especially iliofemoral veins, popliteal veins). Thrombus dislodges → travels through IVC → right heart → pulmonary arteries. Saddle embolus: lodges at bifurcation of pulmonary trunk → sudden death. Risk factors: immobilization, surgery, pregnancy, OCP, malignancy, obesity, thrombophilia. Other emboli: fat (long bone fracture), air (venous access), amniotic fluid (labor). Reference: Robbins — Pulmonary Embolism.

questions.questionText: DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Widespread activation of coagulation → microthrombi AND consumption of clotting factors/platelets → bleeding
questions.explanation: DIC: paradox of simultaneous thrombosis AND hemorrhage. Widespread activation of coagulation cascade → microthrombi in vessels → ischemic organ damage + consumption of clotting factors and platelets → severe bleeding. Labs: prolonged PT/aPTT, low platelets, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer/FDP, schistocytes on smear (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia). Causes: sepsis (most common), obstetric complications, malignancy, trauma, pancreatitis. Reference: Robbins — DIC.

questions.optionB: Systemic hypoperfusion of tissues due to reduced cardiac output or blood volume → cellular hypoxia
questions.explanation: Shock: systemic hypoperfusion → inadequate tissue oxygenation → cellular injury. Types: (1) Cardiogenic (pump failure — MI, tamponade), (2) Hypovolemic (volume loss — hemorrhage, dehydration), (3) Septic/distributive (vasodilation — sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic), (4) Obstructive (PE, tension pneumothorax). Stages: compensated (tachycardia, vasoconstriction) → decompensated (hypotension, lactic acidosis) → irreversible (organ failure, death). Septic shock: most common cause of death in ICU. Reference: Robbins — Shock.

questions.questionText: Edema caused by increased hydrostatic pressure is seen in:
questions.optionB: Congestive heart failure and portal hypertension (ascites)
questions.explanation: Edema: accumulation of excess fluid in interstitial spaces. Increased hydrostatic pressure: CHF (systemic venous congestion → peripheral edema, left heart failure → pulmonary edema), portal hypertension (ascites), DVT (leg edema). Decreased oncotic pressure: nephrotic syndrome (albumin loss), liver failure (decreased albumin synthesis). Increased permeability: inflammation, burns, anaphylaxis. Lymphatic obstruction: filariasis (elephantiasis), post-mastectomy, tumor invasion. Reference: Robbins — Edema.

questions.questionText: Fat embolism syndrome is most commonly associated with:
questions.optionB: Long bone fractures (especially femur) and orthopedic procedures
questions.explanation: Fat embolism syndrome: fat globules enter circulation after long bone fractures (femur, tibia, pelvis). Occurs 1-3 days post-fracture. Classic triad: (1) Respiratory distress (dyspnea, tachypnea — ARDS), (2) Neurological symptoms (confusion, altered consciousness), (3) Petechial rash (characteristic — upper body, conjunctivae). Pathogenesis: mechanical obstruction + free fatty acids causing endothelial damage. Fat globules in urine/sputum. Treatment: supportive. Often confused with PE. Reference: Robbins — Fat Embolism.

questions.optionB: Area of ischemic necrosis due to occlusion of arterial supply or venous drainage
questions.explanation: Infarction: localized area of ischemic necrosis caused by occlusion of arterial supply (most common) or venous drainage. Types: White (anemic/pale) infarcts: solid organs with single (end-arterial) blood supply — heart, kidney, spleen. Red (hemorrhagic) infarcts: dual blood supply or venous occlusion — lung, liver, intestine, brain (reperfusion). Shape: wedge-shaped with apex at occlusion and base at organ periphery. Reference: Robbins — Infarction.

questions.questionText: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare obstetric emergency that presents with:
questions.optionB: Sudden dyspnea, hypotension, DIC, and seizures during labor or shortly after delivery
questions.explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism: amniotic fluid (containing fetal squamous cells, mucin, lanugo, fat) enters maternal circulation via uterine veins → pulmonary microvasculature obstruction + anaphylactoid reaction → acute respiratory failure, cardiovascular collapse, DIC (due to thromboplastin-like substance in amniotic fluid). Mortality: 20-40%. Risk factors: tumultuous labor, advanced maternal age, AROM. Diagnosis: clinical + fetal squamous cells in pulmonary vasculature (autopsy). Reference: Robbins — Amniotic Fluid Embolism.

questions.questionText: Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited cause of:
questions.explanation: Factor V Leiden: point mutation (Arg506Gln) in factor V → resistant to inactivation by activated protein C (APC) → factor Va persists → continues to generate thrombin → hypercoagulable state. Most common inherited thrombophilia (~5% of Caucasians heterozygous). Increases DVT/PE risk 5-fold (heterozygous), 50-fold (homozygous). Other inherited thrombophilias: prothrombin G20210A mutation, antithrombin III deficiency, protein C/S deficiency. Reference: Robbins — Thrombophilia.

questions.questionText: Lines of Zahn are seen in:
questions.optionB: Antemortem (true) thrombi — alternating layers of platelets/fibrin and RBCs
questions.explanation: Lines of Zahn: alternating pale (platelet/fibrin) and dark (RBC) layers in true thrombi → indicates formation in flowing blood (antemortem). Absent in post-mortem clots (homogeneous, non-laminated, not attached to vessel wall). Helps distinguish true thrombus from post-mortem clot at autopsy. Arterial thrombi: form at sites of endothelial injury, grow retrograde, platelet-rich (white thrombus). Venous thrombi: form at sites of stasis, grow in direction of flow, RBC-rich (red thrombus). Reference: Robbins — Thrombus Morphology.
